Funder(s): Mayor of London, Bloomberg Philanthropies; Vodafone consortium (2025+)
Primary Goals: Hyperlocal real-time AQ data for London; public engagement; ULEZ evaluation; school/hospital alerts
Notes / Key Findings: Largest UK LCS network. Vodafone-led consortium from 2025 (Airly, Ricardo, CERC, Cambridge). Sensors £2,095 install + £995/yr. Community programme gives 60 free sensors. 10 sensors on Oxford Street for pedestrianisation monitoring.

Owner / Operator: LB Hammersmith & Fulham / Vortex IoT (Marston Holdings)
Primary Goals: Borough-wide hyperlocal AQ; traffic management; pollution hotspot identification; 'greenest borough' ambition
Notes / Key Findings: Claims densest AQ sensor network globally at 80 sensors/sq mi. AI-powered, mesh network (no SIM). Linked to traffic congestion reduction scheme. Award-winning.

Primary Goals: Region-wide PM monitoring; WHO threshold alerts; Clean Air Plan alternative to CAZ; public health warnings
Notes / Key Findings: 4-year contract worth £550k with EarthSense. First region-wide PM monitoring outside London. Text/email/voicemail alerts when WHO thresholds exceeded. Sensors at schools, hospitals, sports centres.

Funder(s): Defra Air Quality Grant; Carbon Neutral Fund
Pollutants Measured: NO2, NO, O3, CO, CO2, SO2, H2S, PM1, PM2.5, PM10
Primary Goals: One of largest UK real-time networks; 12 AQMAs monitoring; port emissions; solid fuel burning ('Cosy Killer' campaign)
Notes / Key Findings: 40 in Brighton & Hove + sensors in Horsham and Newhaven. Comprehensive pollutant suite including H2S and SO2 (port-relevant). Real-time 24/7 public portal.

Primary Goals: County-wide AQ policy evidence; EarthSense's 2nd largest UK network
Notes / Key Findings: 3-year contract with renewal options. Part of EarthSense's managed network service model.

Owner / Operator: Neath Port Talbot Council / Vortex IoT
Primary Goals: Industrial emissions monitoring (near Tata Steel); community-level pollution mapping; intervention targeting
Notes / Key Findings: First of its kind in Wales. Pilot study on lampposts in residential areas near steelworks. Part of Decarbonisation and Renewable Energy (DARE) Strategy. High-density around Taibach, Aberavon, Sandfields.

Owner / Operator: Swansea Council / Vortex IoT / Swansea University
Primary Goals: Smart city AQ + parking detection; connected to CCTV network
Notes / Key Findings: Innovate UK-funded. Vortex also a supplier in Welsh Government Well-Being Wales programme. Uses existing CCTV infrastructure for backhaul.

Owner / Operator: University of Leeds (Jim McQuaid) / Leeds City Council / WYCA
Funder(s): Defra Air Quality Grant (£220k for PIIP); University of Leeds
Data Availability: PurpleAir map (public); research data via University
Primary Goals: Research: domestic burning patterns, degree-day analysis; Council: public health messaging, West Yorkshire dashboard
Notes / Key Findings: 4+ years of 2-min resolution data. Key research finding: 7pm domestic burning peak distinct from traffic. North Leeds 'Golden Triangle' = high EV + high log burner. PIIP extends to West Yorkshire-wide dashboard. Jim McQuaid & Kirsty prominent in AQ Stripes comms.

Pollutants Measured: PM1, PM2.5, PM10 (size-binned), NO2, temp, humidity
Data Availability: Open data via Geographic Data Service (data.geods.ac.uk)
Primary Goals: Research: urban AQ characterisation; source apportionment potential; bus route exposure
Notes / Key Findings: Solar/battery powered, no mains needed. Size-binned PM data (all OPC-N3 bins). Linear deployment along 10A bus route (2023). Up to 3 years of historic data. Aeternum is a sensor manufacturer based in Liverpool and Dulles, VA.

Primary Goals: Borough-wide real-time AQ; public engagement; policy evidence
Notes / Key Findings: One of larger London borough deployments outside Breathe London. Airly sensors are MCERTS-certified for indicative PM monitoring.

Primary Goals: School AQ awareness; #TimeToAct campaign; behaviour change
Notes / Key Findings: Educational signage at schools alongside sensors. Part of council's AQ awareness campaign targeting children and parents.

Primary Goals: Commercial distribution; public sector AQ monitoring
Notes / Key Findings: AcSoft is UK integrator/distributor. 200+ sensors deployed, sales doubling. Expanding into public sector and community-focused projects.

Primary Goals: Indicative screening for LAQM
Notes / Key Findings: Small indicative network. Not reference-grade. Used for screening as per Defra LAQM guidance (FAQ 140/148).

Primary Goals: Short-term AQ studies; real-time indicative monitoring
Notes / Key Findings: Small deployment for targeted studies rather than permanent network.

Owner / Operator: RSSB / Network Rail / Train Operating Companies
Sensor Type / Brand: AQMesh pods (at selected stations); diffusion tubes (all stations)
Pollutants Measured: NO2, PM1, PM2.5, PM10, O3, CO, SO2, CO2
Data Availability: RSSB reports; some data via station plans
Primary Goals: Rail-specific AQ evidence; DfT policy compliance; station air quality plans (all by end 2026)
Notes / Key Findings: UK's first dedicated rail AQ monitoring programme. Originally 105 stations, reduced to 72 as many showed good AQ. AQMesh pods at selected stations for continuous data. 'Generally good' findings but some concerns at enclosed stations. Extended to 2030.

Pollutants Measured: PM2.5, PM10; some: temp, humidity, noise
Data Availability: Open data — sensor.community; API; archived datasets
Primary Goals: Citizen science democratisation of AQ data; open data; community empowerment
Notes / Key Findings: Formerly Luftdaten.info. Global network (35,000+ stations worldwide). UK presence concentrated in cities. Very low cost (£30-50 DIY). Variable data quality — no co-location calibration. European FILTER QC system applied to archive (13.2B observations). Bristol has active Sensor.Community cluster feeding into city dashboard.

Owner / Operator: UC Berkeley / University of Strathclyde / Glasgow City Council
Funder(s): Research funding (UC Berkeley, Stanford); Met Office; NPL
Pollutants Measured: CO2, CO, NO, NO2, O3, PM2.5
Primary Goals: GHG + AQ monitoring; urban emissions research; COP26 legacy
Notes / Key Findings: ~2km spacing. Part of global BEACO2N network (also Bay Area, LA, Providence). Launched for COP26. Uniquely measures CO2 alongside AQ pollutants. International collaboration (Berkeley, Stanford, Strathclyde, Met Office, NPL).

Owner / Operator: University of Birmingham (Pope, Bousiotis, Harrison, Beddows)
Sensor Type / Brand: Various LCS (OPC-based PM sensors); mobile platforms
Primary Goals: Low-Cost Source Apportionment (LoCoSA) methodology development; 100m-grid pollution mapping; citizen science SA
Notes / Key Findings: Pioneering LoCoSA concept — using LCS for real-time source apportionment. Published in npj Climate and Atmospheric Science (2023), Science of Total Environment (2025). Mobile deployments achieving 100m-grid resolution. Strong connection to METIS/SLS work. Also deploying in Kampala, Uganda.

Owner / Operator: University of York / University of Manchester / Imperial College London
Sensor Type / Brand: AQMesh, Aeroqual AQY, QuantAQ ARIsense, PurpleAir, EarthSense Zephyr
Pollutants Measured: NO, NO2, O3, CO, CO2, PM1, PM2.5, PM10
Data Availability: Open data — CEDA archive; Zenodo; Shiny app
Primary Goals: Systematic long-term evaluation of commercial LCS; PAS 4023 guidance development
Notes / Key Findings: Most extensive open-access LCS evaluation globally. 3 years, 3 sites, 14 manufacturers. Led to PAS 4023:2024 (LCS selection/deployment/QA guidance). Key finding: performance varies hugely between manufacturers and pollutants. Open dataset on CEDA and Zenodo.

Owner / Operator: University of Manchester / Imperial College / University of Birmingham
Primary Goals: National AQ research infrastructure; LCS co-location and calibration; urban pollution characterisation
Notes / Key Findings: Part of UKRI Strategic Priorities Fund. Critical national infrastructure for LCS calibration and validation. Manchester supersite at Fallowfield. London ERG at Imperial. Birmingham at university campus.

Owner / Operator: Now Wireless / Staffordshire CC / Connected Places Catapult
Primary Goals: Dense urban network evaluation; local vs regional PM source separation
Notes / Key Findings: 14-month deployment around ring road. Spectral analysis showed 54% of NO2 is local vs 38% of PM2.5. Avg NO2: 17.5 µg/m³, PM2.5: 8.1 µg/m³. Key paper: 'Are dense networks really useful?' (ACP 2022).

Pollutants Measured: PM2.5, NO2, CO2, VOCs, temp, humidity
Primary Goals: Indoor/outdoor AQ in homes; children's asthma exposure; household-level sensor network
Notes / Key Findings: Unique focus on indoor residential AQ at scale. 100+ homes with children with asthma. Combines indoor and outdoor monitoring. Results expected late 2024 / early 2025.

Primary Goals: Citizen science AQ sensing; open-source hardware; community engagement
Notes / Key Findings: Student-led citizen science project. Open-source sensor designs. Mobile campaigns and community deployments. Focus on democratising AQ data.

Primary Goals: Leveraging BT telecom infrastructure for AQ monitoring; smart city services
Notes / Key Findings: BT partnership uses existing telecom infrastructure for sensor mounting and connectivity. Part of BT smart city offering.

Pollutants Measured: NO, NO2, O3, CO, SO2, CO2, PM1, PM2.5, PM4, PM10, TPC
Primary Goals: Regulatory screening; research; industrial; rail; construction
Notes / Key Findings: UK-designed and manufactured (Stratford-upon-Avon). 500+ customers globally. Used in QUANT, RSSB rail network, school monitoring, industrial. 5-year warranty. Higher cost than basic LCS but more comprehensive pollutant suite.

Owner / Operator: Clean Air for Bristol (community group) / citizens
Data Availability: sensor.community + Bristol city dashboard (Jean Golding Institute)
Primary Goals: Citizen science; CAZ evaluation; community empowerment
Notes / Key Findings: Active community group. Data feeds into Bristol city dashboard alongside Defra and council data. Jean Golding Institute (University of Bristol) developed real-time traffic + AQ dashboard integrating citizen data.

Geography: East Renfrewshire, North Lanarkshire, Glasgow (schools across Scotland)
Owner / Operator: SEPA / local authorities (East Renfrewshire Council, North Lanarkshire Council)
Sensor Type / Brand: AQMesh pods (3 in East Renfrewshire); low-cost PM sensors for schools
Data Availability: School-level data; SEPA reporting; community displays
Primary Goals: School-gate AQ evidence for behaviour change; anti-idling campaigns; active travel promotion; citizen science education (1,200 pupils, 50 classes in 2024)
Notes / Key Findings: Pioneer programme. SEPA were early UK adopters of LCS for schools. Beat the Street initiative (£50k grant). Digital signage at school gates. Community Safety Officers patrol school gates 10hrs/week. Part of Cleaner Air for Scotland 2 (CAFS2) strategy delivery.

Geography: Stirlingshire villages: Aberfoyle, Doune, Deanston, Fintry, Kippen, Thornhill
Scale (No. Sensors): Lamppost-mounted monitors + resident-deployed portable sensors (6 villages)
Owner / Operator: University of Stirling / SEPA / Stirling Council
Sensor Type / Brand: Lamppost-mounted LCS + portable PM sensors (loaned to residents)
Data Availability: Public visualisations via SEPA; community workshops
Primary Goals: Community empowerment; rural AQ evidence; solid fuel burning detection; behaviour change in villages
Notes / Key Findings: Novel rural deployment — most UK LCS networks are urban. One-month rotation per village. Detected solid fuel heating emissions and local pollution events. Residents borrowed sensors to distribute across villages. Community workshops with data visualisation. Model for rural AQ citizen science.

Scale (No. Sensors): AQ sensors within 1,000+ sensor digital observatory
Owner / Operator: University of Stirling / SEPA / Stirling & Clackmannanshire councils
Funder(s): UK Government (£5m) + Scottish Government (£17m) via City Region Deal
Data Availability: Interactive AQ dashboard (launched Jan 2025); open data
Primary Goals: Catchment-scale digital observatory; real-time environmental monitoring; 'living laboratory' for Forth region
Notes / Key Findings: £22m total funding. First catchment-scale digital observatory in UK. AQ dashboard launched January 2025. 5G-enabled. Covers indoor + outdoor AQ. Also deployed sensors on SS Sir Walter Scott (Scotland's oldest steamship). Part of Scotland's International Environment Centre at Stirling.

Network Name: Scotland LEZ Monitoring (Glasgow, Edinburgh, Dundee, Aberdeen)
Geography: Glasgow, Edinburgh, Dundee, Aberdeen city centres
Scale (No. Sensors): Reference monitors + diffusion tubes (4 cities); some LCS
Owner / Operator: Glasgow CC, Edinburgh CC, Dundee CC, Aberdeen CC / SEPA
Funder(s): Scottish Government / Transport Scotland / local authorities
Sensor Type / Brand: Reference AURN monitors + diffusion tubes; some co-located LCS
Primary Goals: LEZ compliance monitoring and evaluation; evidence for Scotland's 4 LEZs (enforcement 2023-2024)
Notes / Key Findings: Glasgow LEZ: 34% NO2 reduction within zone (2022-2024), first time meeting all AQ objectives since LAQM established. Edinburgh LEZ enforcement from Jun 2024. Dundee from May 2024. Aberdeen from Jun 2024. Primarily reference-grade but important context for any LCS work in Scotland. Review of monitoring around schools, hospitals, care homes planned.

Primary Goals: Detailed AQ assessment; emissions inventory; dispersion modelling input
Notes / Key Findings: Part of a detailed assessment project (Feb 2021 - Mar 2023) combining Zephyr LCS, emissions inventory, and atmospheric dispersion modelling. NI councils increasingly using LCS but data not published on airqualityni.co.uk. Only identified LCS deployment in Northern Ireland.

Geography: Newcastle and Gateshead, Tyne and Wear
Pollutants Measured: NO2, O3, CO, PM1, PM2.5, PM10
Primary Goals: Smart city digital twin; multi-parameter urban monitoring (AQ, water, noise, traffic, energy); research infrastructure
Notes / Key Findings: UK's first Urban Observatory. Part of a 600+ sensor network monitoring multiple urban parameters. Over 500 million data points collected. Free, open data. Used by researchers, councils, planners, public. Air Monitors Ltd supplied equipment.

Primary Goals: School-gate exposure assessment; WHO guideline exceedance identification; children's health evidence
Notes / Key Findings: Found several schools exceeded WHO short-term NO2, PM10, and PM2.5 guidelines. All 12 detailed study schools exceeded short-term PM2.5. Published in MDPI Atmospheres. Important evidence base for school streets policies.

Primary Goals: City-wide PM mapping; public engagement in PM2.5 health impacts; LAQM evidence
Notes / Key Findings: Started with 10 Zephyrs, expanded to 23. Contract runs to at least April 2026. All sites below AQ objectives in 2024. Highest PM2.5 annual avg: 8.7 µg/m³ (Blackbird Road). Expanding coverage pattern suggests ongoing commitment.

Data Availability: Visual LED display on-unit; council reporting
Primary Goals: Public-facing real-time AQ display; school-gate and AQMA monitoring; first UK Eco Post deployment
Notes / Key Findings: First Eco Post sensors in the UK. Locations: Caerphilly Road, Malpas Road, High Cross (school), city centre, Pillgwenlly school, Maindee. Visual traffic-light DAQI display on the units themselves. Novel street furniture approach.

Primary Goals: School-based community sensor networks; AQ education extending beyond classroom
Notes / Key Findings: Part of 'Promoting awareness of air pollution in Wales: delivery plan 2025 to 2028'. Welsh Gov will provide opportunities for LAs to bid for monitoring equipment for schools. Aims to build school-based community sensor networks. Policy-driven, not yet deployed.

Owner / Operator: the Environment Centre (tEC) / University of Southampton
Data Availability: Research publication (Vanderwel et al. 2024, Uni Southampton)
Primary Goals: Neighbourhood-level PM2.5 mapping; citizen science; community engagement; solid fuel burning identification
Notes / Key Findings: Jan 2023 - Mar 2024. Found annual average PM2.5 exceeded WHO guidelines. Highest in winter evenings (solid fuel burning). Published by Vanderwel et al. University of Southampton. Community-led with tEC coordination. Model for county-scale citizen science.

Data Availability: Airly Data Platform; LED displays at schools
Primary Goals: School AQ awareness; real-time public display; behaviour change; policy evidence
Notes / Key Findings: Seven sensors at schools and kindergartens. LED colour displays and info panels at sites. Comprehensive pollutant suite including O3. Part of Airly's growing UK schools portfolio alongside Birmingham.

Geography: 8+ ports: Lowestoft, King's Lynn, Hull, Immingham, Troon, Barrow, Newport, Barry, Southampton
Pollutants Measured: NO2, NO, O3, PM1, PM2.5, PM10
Primary Goals: Port sustainability compliance; near-urban community AQ evidence; emissions monitoring
Notes / Key Findings: ABP doubled its network in 2024. EarthSense provides managed service. Covers ports near urban centres (Southampton, Hull, Immingham). Important for port-adjacent community exposure. Growing sector — IMO emissions regulations driving demand.

Owner / Operator: Hutchison Port Holdings / South Coast Science
Pollutants Measured: NO2, SO2, PM1, PM2.5, PM10, temp, humidity
Primary Goals: Port emissions monitoring; SO2 measurement (shipping-relevant)
Notes / Key Findings: Solar-powered, 4G data transmission. Notably includes SO2 (shipping fuel emissions). South Coast Science is a UK company (Hampshire-based). Different sensor platform from ABP/EarthSense — shows sector diversity.

Geography: Heathrow Airport perimeter and flight paths
Owner / Operator: University of Cambridge / Heathrow Airport Ltd
Primary Goals: Airport perimeter AQ characterisation; flight path emission mapping; sensor technology development (led to Alphasense OPC-N2 commercial product)
Notes / Key Findings: Foundational UK LCS project. Rod Jones (Cambridge) led. The sensor designs were commercialised by Alphasense as the OPC-N2 (now OPC-N3), which is now used in Aeternum, AQMesh, and many other devices globally. The SNAQ project essentially seeded the UK LCS hardware ecosystem.

Owner / Operator: University of Surrey (GCARE) / Zero Carbon Guildford
Primary Goals: Real-time public-facing AQ display; citizen science; behaviour change; community empowerment
Notes / Key Findings: Guildford Living Lab collaboration. 5-month study with Zero Carbon Guildford volunteers. Emoji-based public display of pollution levels. Published in Sustainable Horizons. GCARE (Prashant Kumar group) is a major UK AQ research centre. UKRI project ongoing — showcased at Swindon Festival of Tomorrow 2026.

Primary Goals: Open-source sensor design for community replication; citizen science; public engagement with research
Notes / Key Findings: Re-designed sensor system for community self-build. Sent to 3 community groups for testing. Focus on accessibility and replicability. Public engagement with research model. Early stage but interesting open-source approach.

Owner / Operator: Borough Council of King's Lynn & West Norfolk
Primary Goals: Targeted hot-spot monitoring at highest-NO2 location (Railway Road)
Notes / Key Findings: Single sensor at the council's highest-pollution site. Typical of very small councils dipping a toe into LCS. Installed June 2024. Shows EarthSense penetration even at the single-unit level.
